claim: The manuscript uses a null-derived word-construction rule (e.g., a gallows-glyph prefix system) such that a small set of ~5 prefix glyphs accounts for > 60% of all word tokens, producing the observed Zipf exponent of ~0.89 via combinatorial explosion of a limited stem vocabulary
evidence_for: Word-initial glyphs are highly skewed: ['o','c','q','s','d'] dominate
Low Zipf exponent (0.8946) compared to natural language (~1.0â€“1.2) suggests constrained word formation
25 unique glyphs with average word length 5.03 is consistent with prefix+stem+suffix templating
evidence_against: Zipf R-squared of 0.9084 is a moderate fit, not exceptional â€” some natural languages also show exponents near 0.89
A prefix system alone would not explain the high hapax ratio of 70.1%
Glyph entropy 3.86 bits is close to Latin/Italian, suggesting phonemic rather than purely structural encoding
test: For each of the top-5 word-initial glyphs, compute what fraction of all word tokens begin with that glyph; then fit a prefix-stem bigram model and measure how much of the vocabulary is generated by combining those 5 initials with the top-50 word bodies
test_metric: Coverage fraction: (tokens with top-5 initial glyphs) / (total tokens); bigram generative coverage of vocabulary
test_threshold: Coverage > 0.60 and bigram model generating > 50% of unique word types would support a templated prefix system

tests_run.details: The hypothesis predicts frequent prefix-stem combinations, which should reduce hapax ratio; the observed 70.1% hapax ratio contradicts this mechanism. Low Zipf exponent (0.89) provides weak support, but high vocabulary diversity and uniform glyph distribution are inconsistent with 5 glyphs dominating 60% of tokens.

reasoning: If word formation is template-driven rather than phonemic, decipherment attempts based on phoneme-to-glyph mapping will systematically fail; confirming this would redirect effort toward structural rather than substitution models
